issues_found: Source body contains only metadata/abstract, not the full 36-page paper text - verification is limited to metadata accuracy only
Published January 2017 (9+ years old as of 2026-03-31) - exceeds 90-day threshold but qualifies for archival value as historical research
Historical content (1953-1965 Treasury issuance) has indirect rather than direct relevance to current market structure
human_review_reason: Record accurately reflects available source metadata. The key limitation is that the source body only provides metadata/abstract rather than full paper content, but this is sufficient to verify the record's accuracy. Historical research papers typically have legitimate archival value even when content is limited.

corrected_fields.summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 806, January 2017) by Kenneth D. Garbade examines historical Treasury issuance practices, specifically focusing on seven long-term bonds (including one 40-year bond) issued between 1955 and 1963, a period when thirty years was the standard outer boundary of Treasury maturities. The paper traces the circumstances that led to these atypical longer-term issuances.
corrected_fields.why_it_matters: This historical analysis of Treasury market structure (1950s-1960s) provides context on how the Fed and Treasury approached debt maturity management in earlier eras. The occasional issuance of bonds beyond the 30-year boundary may offer historical parallels for understanding current discussions about Treasury issuance practices and market structure evolution.
corrected_fields.macro_context: Historical Treasury debt management analysis covering 1953-1965, a period of post-war monetary policy evolution.
corrected_fields.market_structure_context: Examines the outer boundaries of Treasury bond maturities and the rare issuance of long-term bonds (up to 40 years) before the establishment of regular 30-year issuance patterns in the 1970s.
